coredata.dc:title: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots decorated SnO2 ETL with enhanced charge extraction for efficient perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Gang
Li, Yinghui
Chen, Hanyue
Ke, Lili
Xiong, Jian
Zhou, Conghua
Li, Hongxing
coredata.dc:description: An electron transport layer (ETL) with excellent conductivity and charge extraction ability plays a vital role in accelerating charge extraction and transportation for achieving highly efficient planar perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, amino-rich carbon quantum dots (shortened as ACDs hereafter) with multi-roles are developed and introduced into SnO2 precursor solution to enhance the photoelectric properties of SnO2. First, the conductivity and the Fermi energy level of SnO2 are improved via electron injection from ACDs. With the help of ACDs, the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs is increased from 2.77 × 10−2 mS/cm2 to 6.56 × 10−2 mS/cm2 under illumination, which contribute to efficient charge transport and collection within PSC device. Secondly, the amino functional groups on the surface of ACDs promote the saturated growth of perovskite grain, forming high-quality photoactive layers. When deposited on the SnO2:ACDs, the average gain size of perovskite film is increased from 0.86 μm to 1.1 μm. These outstanding properties greatly accelerate the charge extraction and reduce the trap density within the perovskite layer. As a result, the PSC with ACDs exhibit a champion efficiency of 22.02 % compared with 20.33 % of the control device, and the device stability is also significantly improved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots
Conductivity
Grain growth
Perovskite solar cells

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2020JJ5552 ), Hunan Provincial Education Department Project ( 21A0081 ).

The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Gang Liu reports financial support was provided by Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation. Lili Ke reports financial support was provided by Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department.This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2020JJ5552), Hunan Provincial Education Department Project (21A0081).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of the ACDs. b) TEM image of synthesized ACDs, the inset image is the high-resolution TEM of ACDs. c) UV–vis absorption spectra and d) normalized PL spectra of ACDs. The inset is the picture of the cuvette containing ACDs solution under UV light. e) FTIR spectra of ACDs sample. High-resolution XPS spectra of ACDs f) C1s and g) N1s.

a) High-resolution Sn 3d XPS spectra of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. b) The N 1s XPS spectra of the SnO2:ACDs. c) The FT-IR spectra of the pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. The J-V characteristics of the device FTO/SnO2 or SnO2:ACDs/Ag under d) dark and e) a simulated sunlight (AM 1.5 G). f) J 1/2-V curves of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. The onset and tail of UPS spectra of the pristine g) SnO2 and h) SnO2:ACDs. i) Band level diagram of the completed device based on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs.

a) contact angles of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs substrates. Top-view and cross-sectional SEM images of perovskite films deposited on b) pristine SnO2 and c) SnO2:ACDs. d) XRD patterns and e) absorbance spectra of perovskite films based on different ETLs. f) Dark I–V curves of the electron-only devices with a structure of FTO/ETLs/perovskite/PC60BM/Ag.

a) The designed device configuration. b) Current density-voltage (J-V) curves and c) Steady-state current density and PCE versus time curves for champion PSCs with pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETLs under 1 sun illumination (AM 1.5G). d) The incident-photon-to-charge conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the champion PSCs based SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETL. e) Statistical PCE distribution of PSCs with and without ACDs. f) The long-term stability of the unencapsulated solar cells based on different SnO2 ETLs.

a) Steady-state photoluminescence spectra of perovskite film on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETLs. b) Nyquist plots of the control and optimized device at V = 0.4V in dark. Inset: the equivalent circuit employed to fit the data. c) Mott–Schottky fitting to the capacitance-voltage (C−2-V) plots of the devices. d) Transient photocurrent decay curves and e) transient photovoltage decay curves of PSCs based on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. f) Voltage versus light intensity curves of PSCs based on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs.

head.ce:title: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots decorated SnO2 ETL with enhanced charge extraction for efficient perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory for Micro-Nano Energy Materials and Devices, School of Physics and Optoelectronics, Xiangtan University, Hunan, 411105, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory for Micro-Nano Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Key Laboratory for Micro-Nano Energy Materials and Devices, School of Physics and Optoelectronics, Xiangtan University, Hunan 411105 (P. R. China)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials, School of Materials Science and engineering, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin 541004, (P. R. China)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Super-microstructure and Ultrafast Process, School of Physics and Electronics, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan Province, 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Super-microstructure and Ultrafast Process

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Key Laboratory of Super-microstructure and Ultrafast Process, School of Physics and Electronics, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan Province 410083, (P. R. China)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electron transport layer (ETL) with excellent conductivity and charge extraction ability plays a vital role in accelerating charge extraction and transportation for achieving highly efficient planar perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Herein, amino-rich carbon quantum dots (shortened as ACDs hereafter) with multi-roles are developed and introduced into SnO2 precursor solution to enhance the photoelectric properties of SnO2. First, the conductivity and the Fermi energy level of SnO2 are improved via electron injection from ACDs. With the help of ACDs, the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs is increased from 2.77 × 10−2 mS/cm2 to 6.56 × 10−2 mS/cm2 under illumination, which contribute to efficient charge transport and collection within PSC device. Secondly, the amino functional groups on the surface of ACDs promote the saturated growth of perovskite grain, forming high-quality photoactive layers. When deposited on the SnO2:ACDs, the average gain size of perovskite film is increased from 0.86 μm to 1.1 μm. These outstanding properties greatly accelerate the charge extraction and reduce the trap density within the perovskite layer. As a result, the PSC with ACDs exhibit a champion efficiency of 22.02 % compared with 20.33 % of the control device, and the device stability is also significantly improved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, solar cells based organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite material have been the fastest-growing photovoltaic due to their unique optoelectronic characteristics and low manufacturing cost [1,2]. Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has achieved a certified power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 25.7 % since it was initially reported in 2009, having become a promising candidate for competing with silicon solar cells [3,4]. To date, most of efficient PSCs are structured with planar heterojunction architectures, in which the perovskite layer is usually inserted between the electron transport layer (ETL) and hole transport layer (HTL) to achieve p-i-n or n-i-p configuration [5,6]. Among these functional layers, the electron transport layer (ETL) not only plays multiple roles in transporting electrons and blocking holes but also has an impact on the crystal growth of perovskite thin films. Therefore, the quality of ETL is vital for the enhancement of device performance [7].

Among various ETL materials, SnO2 is a most widely-studied electron transport material in planar PSCs because of its high electron mobility, wide band gap and enhanced UV light stability [7,8]. Nonetheless, SnO2 treated at low temperature suffers deficiencies, such as surface defect, degraded electrical conductivity and poor wetting property with perovskite-precursor solution, which has a negative effect on PCE and stability of PSCs [9]. Moreover, insufficient charge transport still existed at the SnO2/perovskite interface because of the low conductivity of SnO2 or the imperfect energy level arrangement between SnO2 and perovskite layer [10,11]. Therefore, intensive research efforts have been dedicated to enhancing the electronic properties of SnO2 and improving the charge transport at the SnO2/perovskite interface by modifying the surface or adding additives. For example, Yang and co-workers developed an effective ETL with tripled electron mobility by complexing EDTA with SnO2, which, in turn, increased the PCE and stability of PSCs [5]. Zhuang et al. enhanced the electron mobility of SnO2 and charge transfer at the ETL/perovskite interface by introducing RbF into bulk SnO2 [12]. In addition, Wei et al. fabricated an effective ETL by using red-carbon quantum dots to dope SnO2 processed at low temperature, which improved the mobility of electrons by about 20 times, accompanied by an enhanced PCE up to 22.77 % [13].

Not only the electronic properties of the ETL but also its chemical properties and morphology affect the formation of perovskite at the top, which, in turn, affects device performance and stability. As an important part of PSCs, the perovskite films featuring dense packing large crystal size with low defect density are considered to be high-quality that can capture more photons and inhibit non-radiative recombination. Surface modification of ETL with organic molecules has been demonstrated as an effective method to enhance the crystalline and eliminate the bulk defects of perovskite layer [14]. Some modification methods have been proposed, especially for SnO2-based PSCs. For example, Ren et al. introduced ethylammonium bromide (EABr) into the bottom interface of perovskite film to significantly improve the upper perovskite film, resulting in improved photovoltaic performance [15]. Leng et al. modified the surface of ETL with 4,7-Dichloro-1,10-phenanthroline (Cl-phen) to increase the grain size and crystallinity of the perovskite film [16]. Likewise, amino group-containing compounds, such as amino acids and its derivatives [17], amino-functionalized conjugated polymer and isomeric D-π-A molecules [18], have been intensively developed to modify the perovskite layer in PSCs because amino groups can form hydrogen bonds with uncoordinated halide ions on the perovskite surface. As mentioned above, it is complex to optimize the contact between perovskite and ETL, which involves ETL optimization, perovskite film growth and interface energy levels arrangement and so on. It always requires a multi-step complex method to achieve this, which will inevitably increase the complexity of device preparation. One-step technique needs to be developed to improve both ETL and perovskite layer by simultaneously reducing the trap density in perovskite layer and enhancing the charge extraction capacity of ETL.

In this work, a typical hybrid ETL was configured by mixing SnO2 with a multifunctional amino-rich carbon quantum dots (ACDs). The introduction of ACDs to the bulk SnO2 layer possessed multiple advantages. (1) The carbon dots could enhance the conductivity and upshift the Fermi energy level of SnO2 via electron injection, improving the charge transportation at the ETL/perovskite interface. (2) The amino functional groups on the surface of ACDs could promote the growth of perovskite grain, forming high-quality photoactive layers. Upon applying ACDs-assisted SnO2 (SnO2:ACDs) as ETL, the PSC device exhibited significant PCE enhancement relative to the PSCs based on pristine SnO2 ETL. The champion PSC has a PCE of 22.02 % with an open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.11V, a short-circuit current density (J SC) of 24.25 mA/cm2, and a fill factor (FF) of 81.78 %, which proves the positive effects of ACDs on the performance of PSCs.

The SnO2 colloid precursor (tin (IV) oxide,15 wt%) and Lead iodide (PbI2, 99.99 %) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Formamidinium iodide (FAI, 99.5 %), methylammonium chloride (MACl, 99.5 %), methylammonium bromide (MABr, 99.5 %) and 2,2′,7,7-tetrakis[N, N-di(4-methoxyphenyl) amino] −9,9′-spiro-bifluorene (spiro-OMeTAD) were purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. Bis (trifluoromethane) sulfonamide lithium salt (Li-TFSI, 99.95 %) and 4-tert-butylpyridine (4-TBP, 98 %) were purchased from Aldrich. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF,
≥
99.8 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO,
≥
99.9 %), isopropyl alcohol (IPA,
≥
99.8 %), chlorobenzene (CB,
≥
99.8 %) and acetonitrile (AN,
≥
99.8 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Fumaronitrile powder (FN, 98 %) were purchased from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd.

Amino-rich carbon quantum dots (ACDs) were prepared by simple hydrothermal method with fumaronitrile as carbon source. Specifically, 1 g of fumaronitrile was dissolved in 10 mL DI water and 500 μL ammonia water and stirred at 60 °C for 30 min. Whereafter, the solution was subsequently transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 20 min. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained products were collected and washed with deionized water. And then the products centrifugally extracted at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane (LABFIL TER, 0.22 μm pore size). This process was repeated 10 times in order to obtain the ACDs solution. The obtained ACDs were stored in a refrigerator (about 3 °C) for further use.

PSCs devices were fabricated on FTO glass substrates with sheet resistance of 15Ω sq−1 and transmittance of 85 %, which were cleaned with deionized water, acetone, deionized water and ethanol for 20 min successively. After drying by a nitrogen spray gun, FTO substrates were treated with Oxygen plasma cleaning for 20 min. Then, Different ETL precursor solutions were spin-coated on top with speed of 3000 rpm for 30 s, followed by 150 °C annealing in atmospheric environment for 1 h to form compact SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETL. After cooling to room temperature, the samples were plasma cleaned and then transfer into the glove box. The organometal halide perovskite was subsequently deposited by a two-step sequential spin-coating method in a nitrogen glovebox. PbI2 precursor was prepared by dissolving 553 mg PbI2 in mixture solvent between DMF and DMSO (volume ratio of DMF: DMSO = 9.5:0.5, 1 mL), being stirred for overnight at 70 °C. For the preparation of the solution of organic amine salts, 60 mg FAI was dissolved in 1 mL IPA with 8.2 mg/mL MACl and 6 mg/mL MABr. PbI2 solution was spin-coated on the ETL substrate by 2000 rpm for 30 s, and then annealed at 70 °C for 5 min. Then, the organic amine salt solutions were spun cast onto the PbI2 film at 1300 rpm for 30s. After that, the wet films were annealed at 145 °C for 20 min in ambient air (30 % RH) to form perovskite films. After that, the samples were transferred back into a nitrogen glovebox. Subsequently, the spiro-OMeTAD mixture was deposited on top of perovskite film at 3000 rpm for 23s, which prepared by using a 1 ml mixed CB solution including 72.3 mg spiro-OMeTAD, 28.8 μL tBP and 35 μL Li-TFSI acetonitrile solution (260 mg ml−1). Finally, 120 nm Ag was thermally evaporated on the top of spiro-OMeTAD.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of ACDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). UV–vis absorption spectra of the films were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV7500, Pu-Xi Co., Ltd, Shanghai). Fourier infrared spectra of ACDs, SnO2 and SnO2: ACDs were characterized by infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Energy band analysis was characterized by UV photo-electron spectrum (UPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, monochromatized He I source with hν = 21.22 eV). XRD (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15418 nm, D/Max-2500/PC-40KV) was used to characterize the phase. The surface images of perovskite films were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta 250 FEG, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CS Studio5. Wuhan Corrtest Instruments) under a standard illumination and 0.4 V bias condition with a frequency sweeping from 0.1 Hz to 1 MHz and the measured EIS data was fitted using Z-View software. Transient photovoltage and transient photocurrent (TPV/TPC in short) decay curves were measured by a home-made system, which was made of a digital oscilloscope (Keysight DSO-X 3104A, input impedance was 1 MΩ/50 MΩ for TPV/TPC, respectively) and N2 laser (NL100, 337 nm, Stanford). For TPV measurement, background illumination was used to generate open circuit voltage (V OC) of about 1000 mV, then laser pulse was imported to generate perturbation photovoltage which was set to be less than 5 % of the background V OC. External quantum efficiency (EQE) was tested by spectrum performance testing system (7-SCSpec, Beijing) with AC mode. A Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F4700) with excitation wavelength of 380 nm and 532 nm were used to collect steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Morpho-logical properties of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs were characterized by atomic force microscope (AFM 5500, Agilent). The photovoltaic conversion efficiency of the cells is determined by using a class A solar simulator (PT-XE-PWS300 and PT-SUN2S-LH) coupled to a Keithley 2400 source meter. The potential range of forward and reverse scanning during J-V tests is of −0.2 - 1.2 V. The J-V test, IS and EQE measurements were performed under ambient conditions (25 °C and 30 % RH).

ACDs were synthesized according to a previously reported method with minor modifications (Fig. 1 a) [19], and the details can be found in the experiment section. To confirm the successful synthesis of ACDs, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out for the prepared sample. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of ACDs, which revealed that the ACDs exhibits in the form of quasi-zero dimension with an average particle size of about 3 nm. The high-resolution image for ACDs is given in the insert of Fig. 1b. From it a well-resolved crystal lattice with spacing of 0.21 nm was revealed, which is consistent with the (100) lattice fringes of carbon [20]. The UV–vis spectrum for ACDs was shown in Fig. 1c. It can be seen that the UV–vis spectrum exhibited two absorption peaks at 290 nm and 336 nm, which confirms the π–π* and n–π* electronic transitions due to the presence of CC and CO functional group in ACDs [21,22]. Besides, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of ACDs exhibited a strong characteristic absorption band centered at about 400 nm. Next, the corresponding photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ACDs were recorded under excitation of 380 nm. As shown in Fig. 1d, a strong emission from ACDs was observed at 525 nm due to the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs, reflecting the perfect fluorescence characteristic of ACDs. Additionally, the green photoluminescence can be observed obviously in the ACDs solution when suffering from UV illumination with an excitation wavelength of 395 nm.

We next determined the chemical composition of ACDs using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum. From the FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 1e, the peaks observed at around 3270 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration of N–H and O–H. The peak at 1386 cm−1 was due to stretching vibrations of C–N bond, and the peak at around 880 cm−1 was due to the wagging vibration of NH2, suggesting that nitrogen-containing functional groups were successfully linked to the carbon quantum dots structure [20,23]. In addition, the chemical composition and chemical states of elements were further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s was shown in Fig. 1f, and which is deconvoluted into four typical peaks at 284.8, 286.3, 287.0 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to the functional groups C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO, respectively [23]. The detail of the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g) shows the typical peak at 399.5 eV corresponding to the N–H, and the shoulder around 401.8 eV is assigned to the N–C peak [24]. Both of the FT-IR and XPS results were in good agreement, which prove that carbon quantum dots with rich amino groups are successfully prepared.

Since SnO2:ACDs is to be used as ETL in the PSCs, its film formation ability, transmittance and crystallization should be examined. The surface morphologies of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs films could be obtained from the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image. As shown in Fig. S1 a and b, both of pristine SnO2 film and SnO2:ACDs film exhibit a smooth surface with root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of 7.30 nm and 7.28 nm, respectively, indicating that the introduction of ACDs does not change the morphology of the SnO2 film. The optical transmission of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs were shown in Fig. S2 a. Both of two samples exhibit high average transmittance in the visible region, pledging that most light can pass through and be absorbed by the perovskite layer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was also carried out to investigate the effect of ACDs on the crystallization of SnO2 (Fig S2 b). As shown in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, sharp peaks with 2θ values of 26.6°, 38.0° and 51.6° were observed for both of samples, which correspond to the (110), (200) and (211) planes of tetragonal rutile SnO2 crystal, respectively [25]. Moreover, the peaks in the two XRD patterns have no obvious change, indicating that the incorporation of ACDs does not affect the crystallization of SnO2 films. In addition, no obvious peaks of carbon are detected in the XRD patterns of SnO2:ACDs sample, which may be ascribed to the small amounts of the ACDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was used to investigated the interaction between ACDs and SnO2 nanoparticles. Fig. 2 a shows the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of SnO2:ACDs. Compared with pure ACDs sample (Fig. 1g), the N1s peak in SnO2:ACDs does not shift, which indicates that the amino groups does not form chemical bonds with SnO2. Considering that the XPS analyzes only the surface properties, the revealed N1s peak also indicating that the amino groups existed on the surface of SnO2:ACDs film, which is also verified by the subsequent FT-IR results. In Fig. 2b, the spectra of Sn3d can be deconvoluted into peaks of 494.8 eV and 486.4 eV for pristine SnO2 and 495.3 eV and 486.9 eV for SnO2:ACDs, which are in agreement with Sn3d5/2 and Sn3d3/2, respectively. It can be seen that the splitting energy between Sn3d5/2 and Sn3d3/2 peaks is 8.4 eV, indicating the Sn4+ oxidation state in the two samples [26]. Compared with the pristine SnO2 films, the SnO2:ACDs films shows the peaks for Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 shift about 0.4 eV toward higher binding energy. The blue shift indicating the increased electron cloud density around the tin atoms, which could further increased the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs film [27].

FT-IR spectra was used to further evidence the existence of amino groups on the surface of SnO2:ACDs film. As shown in Fig. 2c, the peaks around 557 cm−1 and 673 cm−1 were related to Sn–O–Sn and Sn–O stretching vibration, which is consistent with pure SnO2 sample [28]. Notably, an peak at 880 cm−1, assigning to the symmetric stretching of N–H, was observed in both of pure ACDs and SnO2:ACDs sample, which confirmed the presence of amino groups in the SnO2:ACDs sample [29]. Besides, an additional peak at 1035 cm−1 appeared in SnO2:ACDs sample, which was associated to a combination of Sn–O–Sn and Sn–O–C vibrations, suggesting that the coupling between SnO2 and ACDs is achieved by the formation of the Sn–O–C bond [30,31].

To explore the effect of ACDs on the electrical properties of SnO2 films, we fabricated the devices with the structure of FTO/SnO2 or SnO2:ACDs/Ag, and compared the linear region of the J-V curves under dark and light condition. The conductivity (σ) was calculated using the following equation [12]:
(1)
σ =
I d
V A
where A is the area and d is the thickness of the sample. In Fig. 2d, the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs film under dark is estimated to be 5.26 × 10−2 mS/cm2, which is much higher than that of pristine SnO2 film (2.54 × 10−2 mS/cm2). Meanwhile, the conductivity of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs films under light were calculated to be 2.77 × 10−2 mS/cm2 and 6.56 × 10−2 mS/cm2, respectively (Fig. 2e). Apparently, the light has no obvious effect on the conductivity of pristine SnO2, but significantly enhanced the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs. It is known that the conductivity in SnO2 films can be expressed as σ = q × n × u, where q is the elementary charge, n the number of electrons, and u the electron mobility [32]. Combining the results from Fig. 2d–e, we can see that the ACDs improve the conductivity of SnO2 thin films from two aspects. On the one hand, it improves the electron mobility of the film by passivating the defects within the SnO2 film, which corresponds to the enhancement of conductivity under dark condition. The improved electron mobility was confirmed by a space charge-limited current (SCLC) method. As shown in Fig. 2f, the calculated electron mobility of SnO2:ACDs film was 2.56 × 10−4 cm2V−1s−1, which is apparently higher than that of pristine SnO2 film (1.58 × 10−4 cm2V−1s−1). On the other hand, when SnO2:ACDs thin films are exposed to light, the electron come from the fluorescent ACDs will inject into the conduction band of the SnO2 and further enhance the conductivity of SnO2:ACDs, which was verified by the upshift of Fermi energy level in the UPS results. The higher conductivity for the ETL could also improve the charge transport within the PSC device.

To investigate the change of band positions induced by ACDs incorporation, the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) are performed on both of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs, and the results are shown in (Fig. 2 g-h). From the UPS measurement, the cut-off energy (E cut-off) of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs were determined to be 17.00 eV and 17.18 eV, while the corresponding work function (WF) was calculated to be 4.22 eV and 4.04 eV, respectively, by using the formula of WF = 21.22 - E cut-off (cut-off binding energy). The ACDs incorporation upshift the Fermi energy level of SnO2 by 0.18 eV, a finding we attribute to electron injection from ACDs. Additionally, the valence band (E VB) can be determined by a linear extrapolation of the leading edge of the valence band spectra. The EVB of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs are calculated to be −7.77 eV and −7.71 eV, respectively. Simultaneously, the band gaps of both samples were derived from the absorption spectra and their corresponding Tauc plots (Fig. S3). The band gaps of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs were derived to be about 3.78 eV and 3.79 eV. Then, the conduction band (E CB) were calculated to be −3.99 eV and −3.92 eV for SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs, respectively. With these energy level data of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETLs and others summarized from the references [33], the energy band diagram is shown in the Fig. 2i. It is seen that the E CB of SnO2:ACDs ETL is closer to the E CB of perovskite layer than that of SnO2 ETL, which is beneficial for the enhancement of V OC.

Perovskite films featuring dense packing and large crystal size are vital for the device efficiency and stability. Since the surface energy of the substrate was important for the growth of PbI2 film, the surface energy of the SnO2:ACDs film should be analyzed. To measure the contact angle, PbI2 solution was dropped on the surface of the SnO2 ETL and SnO2:ACDs ETL. Fig. 3 a shows the measured contact angle of the SnO2 sample and SnO2:ACDs sample, 31° and 17°, respectively. The smaller contact angle meaning the lower surface energy, which helps to form smooth and continuous PbI2 film. To further study the effect of ACDs on the growth of perovskite film, we analyzed the morphology and crystal structure of perovskite film deposited on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs substrates. Fig. 3b–c show the top-view SEM images of perovskite film deposited on pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs substrates. The top-view SEM images show that smooth and dense perovskite film were formed on the both of SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. Nevertheless, the average gain size is increased from 0.86 μm for control perovskite film to 1.1 μm for the film deposited on SnO2:ACDs. The larger gain size result in fewer grain boundaries, indicating the lesser recombination centers for the photo-generated charges.

The crystallization of perovskite films was further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement. As shown in Fig. 3d, three distinct diffraction peaks located at around 13.9°, 24.2° and 28.1° are indexed to the (001), (111) and (002) planes, respectively [34]. Obviously, the main diffraction peak of (111) is enhanced for the perovskite film deposited on the SnO2:ACDs, suggesting a better-grown film with improved crystallinity, which is in agreement with the SEM results. Fig. 3e shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the perovskite films deposited on different ETLs. From it we can see that the sample deposited on SnO2:ACDs show slightly higher absorbance than that grow on pristine SnO2 in the whole visible spectrum from 400 to 800 nm, which further confirming the high quality of the perovskite film prepared on the SnO2:ACDs. The results of SEM, XRD and UV–vis absorption spectra demonstrates that the perovskite film based on SnO2:ACDs has higher quality than the one based on pristine SnO2.

Furthermore, the trap densities in perovskite layers were estimated by the space charge limited current (SCLC) method. With a structure of FTO/ETLs/perovskite/PC60BM/Ag, the current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of electron-only devices were measured under dark condition (Fig. 3f). In the I–V profiles, the trap-filled limit voltage (V TFL) is the intersection of the ohmic contact and trap-filled limit region, which reflects the completely filled trap state density. The electron trap density (N t) can be deduced from V TFL according to Equation (2) [35]:
(2)
N t
=
2
ε r
ε 0
V
T F L
q
L 2
Where q is the elementary charge of the electron (q = 1.6 × 10−19 C), L is the thickness of the perovskite layer,
ε 0
is the dielectric constant of the perovskite layer, and
ε r
is the dielectric constant of vacuum (8.85 × 10−14 F/cm). The
N t
of the device with SnO2:ACDs (2.84 × 1015 cm−1) is lower than the value of the control device (6.56 × 1015 cm−1). Above results of SEM, XRD, UV–vis absorption spectra and SCLC demonstrates that the perovskite film based on SnO2:ACDs has higher quality than the one based on pristine SnO2, which is helpful for the improvement of PSCs performance. A plausible explanation to the improved quality of perovskite films based on SnO2:ACDs includes: 1) amino functionalization improves the wettability of PbI2 solution on ETL, and this benefit for the growth of PbI2 film via lowering the surface energy for heterogeneous nucleation, and 2) the amino groups existed on SnO2 film may coordinate via the lone-pair electrons of the nitrogen atoms with the Pb2+ ions of PbI2, leading to retarded perovskite crystal growth [36].

To investigate the effect of ACDs on the performance of PSCs, we fabricated the complete device with the structure of FTO/ETL/FA x MA1-x Pb(Cl x I1-x )3/HTL/Ag to investigate the device performance (Fig. 4 a). The performance parameters, including open-circuit voltage (V OC), short circuit current density (J SC), fill factor (FF) and PCE, for devices made using different amounts of ACDs are summarized in Fig. S4. The best device performance is achieved when the ACDs concentration of 3 wt% is utilized, and an average PCE of 21.05 % was achieved. Fig. 4b shows the J-V characteristics of champion PSCs. The PSCs with SnO2:ACDs ETL displayed an encouraging PCE of 22.02 % with a V OC of 1.11V, J SC of 24.25 mA/cm2, and FF of 81.78 %, which is much higher than the performance of control PSCs with PCE of 20.33 % (V OC = 1.08V, J SC = 23.96 mA/cm2, FF = 78.75 %, respectively). The higher J SC and FF in the device based on SnO2:ACDs ETL were mainly due to the improved perovskite film and the enhanced conductivity of ETL, and the improvement in V OC mainly results from the well-aligned energy level between ETL and perovskite layer. In addition, the SnO2:ACDs based device shows decreased hysteresis in J-V curves with different scanning direction, while the bare SnO2 based device exhibits an obvious hysteresis. The maximum power point (MPP) output of these devices were tested under bias voltage of 0.91 V and 0.93 V for pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs based PSCs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, the PSC based on pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs yielded a stabilized PCE of 19.58 % and 21.65 %, respectively, which were recorded after 150 s light soaking and are comparable to the PCE obtained from the J-V curves. The IPCE curves and the corresponding integrated current density are shown in Fig. 4d. It can be observed that the spectrum is elevated in the 350–650 nm range, indicating higher extraction efficiency of the ACDs-containing device. The integrated current density are 23.22 mA/cm2 and 24.08 mA/cm2 for pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs based PSCs, which match well with the J SC value of the J-V curves in the Fig. 4b.

Stability and repeatability are also very important characteristics for PSCs. Fig. 4e shows the PCE distribution histograms for devices with pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs. As shown in the Fig. 4e, both of devices based on pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs were exhibit excellent repeatability with narrow PCE distribution. Fig. 4f shows the standardized PCE measured by storage time. These PSCs have good storage stability, and devices prepared on SnO2:ACDs have higher stability than SnO2. After 50 days of dark storage at room temperature, the initial efficiency of SnO2 based devices remained at 48 %, while the initial efficiency of SnO2:ACDs remained at 88 % under the same storage conditions. The improved stability might be due to the reduced defects and increased grain size.

In order to gain insight into the charge transport, the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) was used to study the charge transfer process in the PSCs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a reduction in PL intensity was observed in the SnO2:ACDs-based sample, indicating that more efficient electron transfer from perovskite layer to the SnO2:ACDs ETL. Meanwhile, the electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to obtain further information about the effect of ACDs on the charge transfer process. Fig. 5b shows the Nyquist plots of the devices using SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs as ETLs, which are measured at 0.4 V bias under dark condition and fitted with the equivalent circuit shown in inset of Fig. 4e. The circular arc at high frequency region is the signature of the transfer resistance (R tr) and the circular arc at low frequency region is associated with the recombination resistance (R rec). According to the EIS results, the PSC with SnO2:ACDs exhibits the smaller R tr and larger R rec than the PSC with pristine SnO2. In this study, the only difference is in ETL thus it can be assumed that the smaller R tr is attributed to an improved interfacial contact between ETL and perovskite layer. Both of EIS and PL results indicated that the SnO2:ACDs ETL possesses the more efficient charge extraction ability. To better understand the enhancement of charge transport at the SnO2:ACDs/perovskite interface, Mott-Schottky (M − S) analysis was performed. Fig. 5c shows the 1/C2–V curves of the devices based on SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs ETLs. The PSC based on SnO2:ACDs exhibits a higher built-in voltage (V bi) of 0.93 V than that based on SnO2 (0.89 V). The significantly improved V bi implies an enhanced driving force for the separation of photogenerated carriers, which is favorable for achieving a higher V OC [37].

To understand the detailed mechanism about the charge transfer and recombination properties, we performed transient photocurrent (TPC) and transient photovoltage (TPV) tests to calculate the charge-recombination lifetime (τ r) and charge-extraction lifetime (τ e), respectively. Fig. 5d shows the photocurrent decay of samples based on pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs measured under short circuit state. The TPC exhibited a τ e of 4.23 μs for SnO2:ACDs-based device, faster decay than the device based on pristine SnO2 (5.98 μs). The lower τ e was further evidence that the charge extraction and transport process carried on more effective in the SnO2:ACDs based device, which is instrumental in the enhancement of photocurrent. Fig. 5e shows the TPV analysis of PSCs with pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs under open-circuit condition, respectively. The PSCs with SnO2:ACDs shows a longer τ r of 6.46 μs compared to the pristine SnO2-based PSC (4.34 μs). This is consistent with the enhanced device parameters especially for FF and V OC observed in the J-V measurements, and it further proves that the introduction of ACDs could efficiently improve the perovskite film and suppress the charge recombination.

Recombination mechanism of photogenerated carriers in PSCs were further explored in detail by V OC-light intensity curve. Fig. 5f shows the V OC vs light intensity curves of PSCs with and without ACDs. The deviation between the slope and kBT/q reflects the trap-assisted recombination. Clearly, the device with ACDs shows a weaker V OC dependence on the light intensity (with a slope of 1.67 kBT/q) compared to that of the device without ACDs (with a slope of 1.96 kBT/q). The weaker V OC dependence on the light intensity further confirmed that the trap-assisted Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) recombination was inhibited in the SnO2:ACDs-based PSCs. Combined, all the results confirm that SnO2:ACDs is the most effective ETL for the planar PSCs.

In conclusion, we introduced an amino-rich carbon quantum dots into SnO2 to enhance the electrical properties of SnO2 ETL and improve the ETL/perovskite interface. Benefiting from the high fluorescence of ACDs, the conductivity and band energy level of SnO2:ACDs ETL were improved, which enhanced the charge extraction at the ETL/perovskite interface. In addition, the amino functional groups existed on the surface of ETL significantly improved the film quality of perovskite layer, which reduced the grain boundary and trap-state density of perovskite film and suppress the charge recombination. Finally, the V OC and FF of the PSCs based on the SnO2:ACDs were improved, resulting in a high PCE of 22.02 %. A much higher performance is expected for SnO2:ACDs based PSCs after further optimizations in future studies. This study paves a new road to improve the performance of the PSCs, and provides a reference for practical applications.

Gang Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Founding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yinghui Li: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Hanyue Chen: Investigation, Software. Lili Ke: Founding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jian Xiong: Data curation, Resources. Conghua Zhou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Hongxing Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

See the supplementary information for the device preparation and characterization methods. AFM height images of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs (Fig. S1). Transmission spectra and XRD patterns of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs samples (Fig. S2). The Tauc plot of pristine SnO2 and SnO2:ACDs films (Fig. S3). Distribution of performance parameters of PSCs with different ACDs concentration (Fig. S4).
